oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Assessment of water quality and of seasonal water use on feedlot and grazing operations in western Kansas operating within the High Plains aquifer
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Theurer, Miles E.
Waggoner, Justin W.
Ensley, Steven M.
Fritz, Scott A.
Depenbusch, Brandon E.
Fox, J. Trent
McCarty, Travis M.
McCollum, Ryan M.
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: beef production
water intake
water conservation
land resource
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: The beef cattle industry is the leading sector of the agricul­ture economy in Kansas. The objectives of this study were to evaluate the water quality of Kansas grazing and feedyard water samples, as well as quantify feedyard water use by month for 10 feedyards in western Kansas. Sixteen grazing sites in western Kansas were selected to collect forage and water samples. Forage samples from each grazing site were collected in August 2024. Forage clippings from each opera­tion, which together constituted a representative sample of the pasture, were collected and sent to a feed analysis labora­tory. Monthly water well meter readings were evaluated for nine of the feedyards as one feedyard did not have accurate historical information. Data were evaluated from January 2020 through June 2025. Water pH (P < 0.01) and sulfate (P = 0.06) were significantly associated with sampling month for the grazing operations. For the feedyard water quality analy­ses, water pH (P < 0.01), sulfate (P < 0.01), phosphorus (P = 0.01), calcium (P < 0.01), chromium (P < 0.01), iron (P < 0.01), cobalt (P < 0.01), selenium (P < 0.01), and molybdenum (P < 0.01) were all associated with sampling month; however, magnitude of differences was small by month. Maximum average water use was 14.69 gallons/head/day. Average monthly high tempera­ture was positively associated (P < 0.01) and ration dry matter negatively associated (P = 0.02) with feedyard water use. These results provide a baseline for benchmarking water conserva­tion efforts.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: The Bovine Practitioner; Vol. 60 No. 2 (2026): Online First; 21-27
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
